text: The Digital Divide in the Ecuadorian Countryside: The Situation regarding Access to and Use of Information and Communication Technologies (ICTs) This study, which gives an in-depth look into the digital divide in the Ecuadorian countryside and presents the current situation regarding access to and the use of ICTs, reveals a complex situation characterized by huge structural inequalities and opportunities. According to figures from recent surveys, only 67% of urban households in Ecuador have Internet access, whereas in rural areas only 27% of households have Internet access, although the lack of hardware and lack of a good quality network also form a barrier. Although the State Programme “Ecuador Digital” is part of the public policies aimed at increasing access to the Internet in rural areas, this programme has also presented barriers, specifically related to its sustainability and the geographical reach. Additionally, the projects implemented in order to achieve digital literacy have had varied results, and in this respect it is important to highlight that they were highly effective in those cases in which they were accompanied by complementary aspects such as local support in the form of training, etc. Furthermore, access to technologies in rural areas is still blocked by huge obstacles deriving from an economy that is insufficient to be able to access new goods and services and from the social acceptance which is not yet sufficient, although it is growing, based on the use of mobile phones and, recently, in the programmes of social organizations. In order to attend to this important challenge, the commission proposes a series of recommendations in relation to access to technologies in the countryside, and which consist of four specific points, based on the need to increase investment in communications infrastructures, achieve an effective articulation among the different agents in the sector, through the creation of mechanisms for public-private partnership, improve access for the social group most affected by the digital divide through adequate and culturally adapted training, and also include efficient mechanisms of monitoring in the public policies implemented in order to verify their impact and to correct them, where necessary.
